patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: fallback to transaction csum tree on a commit root csum miss

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: fallback to transaction csum tree on a commit root csum miss

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a fallback mechanism in the Btrfs file system to handle cases where the commit root checksum is missing, which can occur when an extent is relocated within a transaction and its checksum is not updated. The patch allows the system to fall back to searching for the checksum in the transaction csum tree, ensuring that data integrity is maintained even in these edge cases. This change addresses a specific issue related to relocation and commit root checksums, which has been observed in production environments.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock, which is a type of lock in the Linux kernel. The goal is to measure and understand the performance impact of inlining unlock operations on modern CPUs with shared L1 instruction caches. Initial benchmarks showed that inlining unlock can cause measurable kernel i-cache pressure, leading to a 1.27% throughput decrease on a workload-bound system. To mitigate this issue, the patch adds a tracepoint to collect data on contended releases, allowing for further analysis and optimization of lock performance.

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/3] mm/numa: reserve standby NUMA nodes for runtime claiming

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/3] mm/numa: reserve standby NUMA nodes for runtime claiming

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 1/3] mm/numa: add exclusive node pool and numa=standby boot parameter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new feature to the Linux kernel's NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) subsystem, allowing for the creation of 'standby' nodes at boot time. These standby nodes can be claimed by drivers at runtime, enabling them to place hotplugged memory on distinct NUMA nodes without requiring BIOS-assigned proximity domains. The patch includes a new exclusive node pool with claim and release interfaces, as well as modifications to the numa_init_standby_nodes() function to rebuild the NUMA distance table for standby nodes. This feature is made possible by a new boot parameter 'numa=standby=<N>', which specifies the number of standby nodes to create. The patch also addresses potential issues with dynamic node addition and phys_to_target_node() ambiguity.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary:
reviewer questioned the necessity of creating standby nodes post-emulation and suggested that dynamic addition of nodes is not trivial due to services relying on num_possible_nodes() as a static value

Reviewer Mike Rapoport noted that adding standby nodes to num_possible_nodes() could skew resource calculations by services like futex_init, which uses this value to calculate hashsize, and requested consideration of this impact.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Tue, Jun 09, 2026 at 09:45:15PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:

---

AFAIU, we don't add the additional nodes for generic hotplug memory but
rather for exclusive use of by drivers/applications that are aware of these
nodes.
Wouldn't adding them to possible nodes actually skew the calculation of the
resources by the services utilizing num_possible_nodes()?

With the futex_init() example, won't be hashsize scaled down two much
because we've added these special nodes to the possible mask?

-- 
Sincerely yours,
Mike.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author clarified that the patch is intended for 'non-generic' hotplug, which would still use the hotplug mechanism but not for generic memory, addressing a concern about its purpose and scope.

Author acknowledged that the use of num_possible_nodes() in futex_init() is problematic and agreed it should not be used, but did not commit to a fix.
review_comments.raw_body:
The intent is to use for "non-generic" hotplug (see the whole private
node series [1]), which would eventually still use the hotplug mechanism
just not for generic memory.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260222084842.1824063-1-gourry@gourry.net/

---

The result is the same as BIOS reserving nodes with PXM entries that
don't get used.  The CXL ACPI Tables do this for CXL Fixed Memory
Windows that may never be hotplugged.

So really i think you're pointing out that futex_init() here probably
shouldn't be using num_possible_nodes?

~Gregory

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 2/3] acpi/numa: add CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_STANDBY_NODES
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_STANDBY_NODES, which allows platforms to reserve empty NUMA nodes at boot for later use by drivers in memory tiering or isolation scenarios. The number of standby nodes to reserve is configurable and can be set between 0 and 16. This feature defers the creation of standby nodes until after NUMA emulation runs, ensuring consistency with existing numbering behaviors. Drivers can then claim these reserved nodes using numa_request_exclusive_node().

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 3/3] acpi/numa: add CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_ADD_CFMWS_NODES
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_ADD_CFMWS_NODES, which allows the number of additional standby NUMA nodes to be reserved per CEDT CFMWS entry. This is useful for CXL drivers that want to place memory on distinct NUMA nodes within the same CXL Fixed Memory Window. The patch also updates the acpi_parse_cfmws function to request any standby nodes created after numa_emulation runs. The number of additional standby nodes to reserve can be set between 0 and 4, with a default value of 0.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This RFC patch series proposes a way to reserve empty 'standby' NUMA nodes at boot, allowing drivers to place hotplugged memory on distinct nodes later at runtime without relying on BIOS descriptions. The approach involves adding three ways to reserve standby nodes: through a boot parameter, an ACPI configuration option, and a CXL Fixed Memory Window (CFMWS) configuration option.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary: Identified potential issues with adding standby nodes to the possible node mask, including skewing resource calculations and hashsize scaling. Suggested reevaluating the approach for non-generic hotplug memory.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Tue, Jun 09, 2026 at 09:45:15PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> It can be at times preferential to logically split up hotplug memory
> capacity into more nodes than are described by BIOS at boot time.
> 
> However, if nodes are not described at __init time, they are not
> possible to add later on.

...
 
> 1) Can we do dynamic addition of nodes?
> 
>    Not Trivially
> 
>    Some services utilize num_possible_nodes() as a static value to
>    calculate the amount of resources to use at runtime (bpf, md/raid5).
> 
>    Example: futex_init uses num_possible_nodes() as part of its
>             hashsize calculation during __init.

AFAIU, we don't add the additional nodes for generic hotplug memory but
rather for exclusive use of by drivers/applications that are aware of these
nodes.
Wouldn't adding them to possible nodes actually skew the calculation of the
resources by the services utilizing num_possible_nodes()?

With the futex_init() example, won't be hashsize scaled down two much
because we've added these special nodes to the possible mask?

-- 
Sincerely yours,
Mike.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfs/localio: fix nfsd_file ref leak on nfs_local_doio() init failure

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfs/localio: fix nfsd_file ref leak on nfs_local_doio() init failure

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a reference leak in the NFS client's localio code, specifically in the nfs_local_doio() function. The bug occurs when the function returns early due to zero-count or iocb initialization failure, causing an nfsd_file reference to be leaked and preventing network namespace teardown and filesystem unmount. The patch adds missing calls to nfs_local_file_put() and introduces a new cleanup label to release resources in case of iocb init failure.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 00/21] nfsd: more bugfixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/21] nfsd: clear opcnt on compound arg release to prevent OOB read
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where stale compound metadata could be exposed to userspace through the status interface. The problem occurred when an error path was taken after setting the operation count (opcnt) to an odd value, causing an out-of-bounds read from adjacent slab memory. To fix this, the patch clears the opcnt field unconditionally in nfsd4_release_compoundargs().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/21] nfsd: add missing read barrier to rpc_status_get dumpit seqcount retry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a missing read memory barrier to the rpc_status_get_dumpit function in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit(). The barrier ensures that all data reads complete before re-checking the status counter, preventing potential issues on weakly-ordered architectures. This fix is necessary because the hand-rolled seqcount-like protocol in this function lacks a read memory barrier, which can lead to inconsistent results and ineffective retry logic.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/21] nfsd: fix netlink dumpit error handling for rpc_status_get
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an error handling issue in the NFS daemon's netlink dump functionality for rpc_status_get. When nla_put operations fail, a partial message is left in the skb without canceling it, causing the netlink dump infrastructure to treat it as a fatal error and abort the entire dump. The patch corrects this by canceling the partial message on all nla_put failure paths and paginating when possible rather than returning a fatal error.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/21] sunrpc: defer rq_argp and rq_resp free until after RCU grace period
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free issue in the sunrpc code by deferring the free of rq_argp and rq_resp until after an RCU grace period, ensuring that RCU readers do not access freed resources. The patch introduces a new callback function svc_rqst_free_rcu to handle the delayed freeing of these resources, while synchronously freeing other resources such as bvec, buffer pages, scratch folio, and auth_data.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/21] nfsd: check nfsd4_acl_to_attr() return value in nfsd4_create()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where ACL translation errors were being silently discarded during file creation. The patch adds an early exit check after nfsd4_acl_to_attr() to ensure that any errors are properly handled and do not get overwritten by subsequent code. This fix ensures that ACLs are correctly translated and applied during file creation, preventing potential security issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/21] nfsd: add filehandle match check to nfsd4_delegreturn()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a filehandle match check to the nfsd4_delegreturn() function, which is a stateful NFSv4 operation that previously did not verify the delegation's file matches cstate->current_fh. This fix prevents clients from DELEGRETURNing with a mismatched filehandle, which could destroy the correct delegation and wake up the wrong inode's waiters.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/21] nfsd: validate nseconds in TIME_DELEG decode paths
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds range checks to prevent a malformed timespec from being stored in the Linux kernel's NFS server implementation. The checks are added to two files, nfs4xdr.c and nfs4callback.c, which handle SETATTR and CB_GETATTR paths respectively. The checks ensure that the nseconds value is less than NSEC_PER_SEC, preventing potential issues with notify_change() propagating malformed timespeces to disk.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/21] nfsd: remove premature NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED in CLAIM_PREVIOUS path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes a premature setting of the NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED flag in the CLAIM_PREVIOUS path of the nfsd kernel module. The flag is set too early, causing it to remain permanently confirmed even if an open operation fails. This fix ensures that the correct success-path setter is used instead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/21] nfsd: fix version mismatch loops in nfsd_acl_init_request()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd_acl_init_request function where it incorrectly checks for supported versions by using rqstp->rq_vers instead of the loop variable i, causing an infinite loop and incorrect results. The fix involves replacing rqstp->rq_vers with i to correctly determine the supported version range. This patch addresses an issue introduced by a previous commit that allowed containers to set supported NFS versions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/21] nfsd: fix FL_SLEEP being set unconditionally for all LOCK types
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where the FL_SLEEP flag was being set unconditionally for all lock types, including non-blocking ones. The fix replaces the bitmask test with explicit equality checks to ensure that FL_SLEEP is only set when necessary. This addresses an issue introduced by a previous commit and improves the accuracy of lock notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/21] nfsd: add fh_want_write() for early-verified SETATTR in nfsd_proc_setattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a call to fh_want_write() in the nfsd_proc_setattr() function, specifically after an early verification of the file handle using fh_verify(). This ensures that notify_change() is called with a mount write reference, which was previously skipped due to the early verification. The fix addresses a problem introduced by a previous patch that disabled a timestamp workaround for NFSv3+.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/21] nfsd: fix clock domain mismatch in clients_still_reclaiming()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a clock domain mismatch in the clients_still_reclaiming() function of the NFS server. The function was using CLOCK_REALTIME to compute a deadline, but comparing it against CLOCK_BOOTTIME, which is always false due to the large difference between these two clocks. To fix this, the patch adds a new boot_time_bt field that uses CLOCK_BOOTTIME and updates the deadline computation to use this new field. This prevents clients from holding the server in grace indefinitely by sending CLAIM_PREVIOUS OPEN requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 13/21] nfsd: use test_and_clear_bit for somebody_reclaimed to prevent lost update
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem in the NFS daemon where a concurrent update could be lost due to a race condition between testing and clearing a flag. The patch replaces separate test_bit() and clear_bit() calls with a single atomic test_and_clear_bit() operation, ensuring that updates are not lost.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 14/21] nfsd: reject reclaim LOCK after RECLAIM_COMPLETE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 server where it incorrectly accepts reclaim LOCK requests after RECLAIM_COMPLETE has been sent by a client. The fix adds a check to ensure that the client's NFSD4_CLIENT_RECLAIM_COMPLETE bit is set before accepting a reclaim request, as required by RFC 8881. This prevents the server from accepting invalid reclaim requests when the grace period is still active.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 15/21] nfsd: validate sockaddr length per family in listener_set
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS daemon's netlink listener set functionality, where a CAP_NET_ADMIN caller could send an invalid sockaddr structure that would cause out-of-bounds reads across multiple consumers. The patch tightens the policy for the NFSD_A_SOCK_ADDR attribute to require at least 16 bytes and adds per-family length validation in both nlmsg_for_each_attr_type loops. This ensures that only valid sockaddr structures are processed, preventing potential security vulnerabilities.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 16/21] lockd, nfsd: RCU-protect nlmsvc_ops dispatch
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a use-after-free bug in the lockd and nfsd modules by RCU-protecting the nlmsvc_ops dispatch. The bug occurs when the nfsd module is removed, causing stale loads of nlmsvc_ops and resulting in either a NULL dereference or a module-text use-after-free. To fix this, the patch declares nlmsvc_ops as __rcu, publishes it via rcu_assign_pointer(), and clears it via RCU_INIT_POINTER() + synchronize_rcu(). Additionally, a struct module *owner field is added to nlmsvc_binding and the module is pinned across indirect calls with try_module_get/module_put. When the binding is torn down, fput() is used to avoid leaking struct file references.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 17/21] nfsd: move nfsd_debugfs_init() after nfsd4_init_slabs() in init_nfsd()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the initialization of NFS debugfs to after slab allocation in the init_nfsd function, ensuring that debugfs files are properly cleaned up when slab allocation fails. The patch also updates the unwind paths for both the init_nfsd error path and exit_nfsd function to match reverse-initialization order, which is a cleanup with no functional change.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 18/21] nfsd: initialize DRC hash table before registering shrinker
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server code where the DRC hash table is not fully initialized before it becomes visible to the shrinker, causing a NULL dereference on weakly-ordered architectures. The fix involves moving the initialization of the bucket list heads and the drc_hashsize store before registering the shrinker. This ensures that the hash table is properly initialized before it can be accessed by the shrinker.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 19/21] nfsd: restore rq_status_counter to even on all nfsd_dispatch() exit paths
series_items.patch_summary: This patch restores the rq_status_counter to an even value on all exit paths of nfsd_dispatch(), ensuring that a lockless reader can detect concurrent mutations in request fields. The counter is initially set to an odd value when a request is decoded, and then reset to an even value after processing. However, previous code only restored the counter to an even value on the fully successful path, leaving it stuck at an odd value on other paths. This patch adds a helper function to advance the counter to the next even value and calls it on every return path that follows the initial odd-valued store. The decode-error path is left untouched as it occurs before the counter is set odd.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 20/21] nfsd: reset thread skip index when advancing pools in rpc_status dumpit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue in the NFS daemon where when resuming a netlink dump of rpc_status, the thread skip count was applied to every pool, not just the current one. As a result, some threads were silently omitted from the dump. The patch resets the thread skip index only for the current pool and starts each subsequent pool from thread 0, ensuring that all threads are included in the dump.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 21/21] nfsd: drop the stateid, not the stateowner, on seqid_op replay retry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 state management code where an incorrect reference was being dropped, leading to a potential use-after-free and refcount underflow issue. The fix involves dropping the correct reference (the stid) instead of the stateowner when retrying a replay operation. This ensures that the stateowner remains alive through its reference held by the stid, mirroring the behavior in the open path.

patches_submitted.patch_summary:
This version is addresses review comments from Sashiko. There wasn't much in the way of regressions in v1, but it did notice a number of nearby problems that should also be fixed.

This adds 3 patches to fix those. I also dropped the localio patch from the series since that should probably go through the NFS client tree. I'll send it separately.

AFAICT, these are garden-variety bugs. Chuck, please consider these for v7.3.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: nfsd4_release_compoundargs() resets args->ops to the inline iops[8] array when the dynamically-allocated ops buffer is freed, but leaves args->opcnt at its original value (which can be up to 200 for NFSv4.1+ compounds). If rq_status_counter is stuck at an odd value (which can happen when nfsd_dispatch() hits an error path after setting it odd), the RPC status dumpit handler reads min(opcnt, 16) entries from args->ops[].
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_release_compoundargs() resets args->ops to the inline iops[8]
array when the dynamically-allocated ops buffer is freed, but leaves
args->opcnt at its original value (which can be up to 200 for NFSv4.1+
compounds).

If rq_status_counter is stuck at an odd value (which can happen when
nfsd_dispatch() hits an error path after setting it odd), the RPC
status dumpit handler reads min(opcnt, 16) entries from args->ops[].
Since iops only has 8 elements and is the last field in struct
nfsd4_compoundargs, reading indices 8-15 accesses adjacent slab memory
and leaks it to userspace via netlink.

Zero opcnt unconditionally in nfsd4_release_compoundargs() so stale
compound metadata is never exposed through the status interface.

Fixes: bd9d6a3efa97 ("NFSD: add rpc_status netlink support")
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

index b9037d99b564..1e4a51926910 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
@@ -6440,6 +6440,7 @@ void nfsd4_release_compoundargs(struct svc_rqst *rqstp)
 		args->ops = args->iops;
 		kvfree_rcu_mightsleep(old_ops);
 	}
+	args->opcnt = 0;
 	while (args->to_free) {
 		struct svcxdr_tmpbuf *tb = args->to_free;
 		args->to_free = tb->next;

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The hand-rolled seqcount-like protocol in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit() is missing a read memory barrier (smp_rmb) before its second counter check.  The standard kernel read_seqcount_retry() includes smp_rmb() to ensure that all data reads complete before the counter is re-checked.
review_comments.raw_body:
The hand-rolled seqcount-like protocol in nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit()
is missing a read memory barrier (smp_rmb) before its second counter
check.  The standard kernel read_seqcount_retry() includes smp_rmb()
to ensure that all data reads complete before the counter is re-checked.

Without this barrier, on weakly-ordered architectures (ARM, POWER),
the CPU may reorder field reads past the second counter check, making
the retry logic ineffective: it could observe a consistent counter pair
while reading fields that have been concurrently modified by the writer.

Add smp_rmb() before the second smp_load_acquire() to match the
barrier semantics of the standard seqcount read-side.

Fixes: ac18892ea3f7 ("NFSD: add rpc_status netlink support")
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c | 6 ++++--
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
index c06d25c06f06..a4b5b1467fe2 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
@@ -1576,9 +1576,11 @@ int nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit(struct sk_buff *skb,
 #endif /* CONFIG_NFSD_V4 */
 
 			/*
-			 * Acquire rq_status_counter before reporting the rqst
-			 * fields to the user.
+			 * Ensure all field reads complete before re-checking
+			 * the status counter. Pairs with the smp_store_release
+			 * in nfsd_dispatch to form a seq-lock like protocol.
 			 */
+			smp_rmb();
 			if (smp_load_acquire(&rqstp->rq_status_counter) !=
 			    status_counter)
 				continue;

-- 
2.54.0

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: nfsd_genl_rpc_status_compose_msg() returns -ENOBUFS on nla_put failure without calling genlmsg_cancel(), leaving a partial message in the skb. The caller then propagates -ENOBUFS directly, which the netlink dump infrastructure treats as a fatal error, aborting the entire dump. The correct netlink dump convention is: - Cancel any partial message with genlmsg_cancel() - If prior messages were added to the skb (skb->len > 0), save the current iterator position and return skb->len to paginate - Only return a negative errno when no messages fit at all Fix compose_msg to cancel the partial message on all nla_put failure paths, and fix the caller to paginate when possible rather than returning a fatal error. Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8 --- fs/nfsd/nfsctl.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: svc_rqst_free() frees rqstp->rq_argp and rqstp->rq_resp synchronously via kfree(), but defers the rqstp struct free via kfree_rcu().  After svc_exit_thread() calls list_del_rcu() and svc_rqst_free(), there is a window where RCU readers that started before list_del_rcu() can still traverse the thread list and find the rqstp.  These readers (e.g. nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit()) dereference rqstp->rq_argp, which has already been freed \u2014 a use-after-free. Fix this by moving the kfree of rq_argp and rq_resp into an explicit call_rcu() callback alongside the struct free.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: nfsd4_create() stores the return value of nfsd4_acl_to_attr() in status, but the switch(create->cr_type) block unconditionally overwrites it in every branch. ACL translation errors are silently discarded, and the CREATE proceeds without the requested ACL.
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_create() stores the return value of nfsd4_acl_to_attr() in
status, but the switch(create->cr_type) block unconditionally
overwrites it in every branch. ACL translation errors are silently
discarded, and the CREATE proceeds without the requested ACL.

Add an early exit check after nfsd4_acl_to_attr(), matching the
pattern already used in nfsd4_setattr().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: nfsd4_delegreturn() is the only stateful NFSv4 operation that does not call nfs4_check_fh() to verify the delegation's file matches cstate->current_fh. A client can DELEGRETURN with a mismatched filehandle, destroying the correct delegation but waking the wrong inode's waiters.
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_delegreturn() is the only stateful NFSv4 operation that does
not call nfs4_check_fh() to verify the delegation's file matches
cstate->current_fh. A client can DELEGRETURN with a mismatched
filehandle, destroying the correct delegation but waking the wrong
inode's waiters.

Add the missing nfs4_check_fh() call after the generation check.

review_comments.summary: nfsd4_open() sets NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED on the openowner before calling do_open_fhandle(), which can fail. If it fails, the openowner stays permanently confirmed despite the OPEN failing. The correct success-path setter already exists in init_open_stateid(). Remove the premature setter. NFSv4.
review_comments.raw_body:
nfsd4_open() sets NFS4_OO_CONFIRMED on the openowner before calling
do_open_fhandle(), which can fail. If it fails, the openowner stays
permanently confirmed despite the OPEN failing. The correct
success-path setter already exists in init_open_stateid().

Remove the premature setter. NFSv4.1+ is unaffected as sessions
always confirm at creation time.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 00/20] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 00/20] nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 01/20] nfsd: check fl_lmops in nfsd_breaker_owns_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd_breaker_owns_lease() function to quickly check if a lease is an NFS daemon (nfsd) lease by verifying that its fl_lmops field matches the nfsd_lease_mng_ops operations. This optimization allows for earlier rejection of non-nfsd leases, improving performance and reducing unnecessary checks. The patch adds a new constant nfsd_lease_mng_ops to the code and updates the function to check for this value before proceeding with further lease verification.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 02/20] nfsd: add protocol support for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds protocol support for CB_NOTIFY in the nfsd kernel module. It updates the xdr (External Data Representation) files to include the necessary definitions and removes duplicate definitions from other headers. The patch also regenerates the xdr files, which caused conflicts with constants defined in the RFC but not part of the kernel API. These constants are removed, and userspace consumers should obtain them from more authoritative sources.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the value of NFS4ERR_EOF can be leaked onto the wire, and suggested keeping the .x document aligned with the specification by defining a 'maximum value' symbolic constant in one of the hand-rolled headers instead of copying it from existing definitions. He also pointed out that NFS4ERR_FIRST_FREE is used to determine the numeric value for NFSERR_EOF.

Reviewer suggested moving 'pragma public' directives to where they're first needed, as their necessity may disappear in subsequent patches.
review_comments.raw_body:
This value can be leaked onto the wire. The basic enum encoder
checks that these values are part of the .x before sticking
them on the wire.

Please keep the .x document aligned with the specification. If
you need a "maximum value" symbolic constant, please define it
in one of the hand-rolled headers. (I guess this one was copied
over from the existing hand-rolled NFS4ERR definitions).

I see that NFS4ERR_FIRST_FREE is used to determine the numeric
value for NFSERR_EOF.

fs/nfsd/nfs3xdr.c:              if (xdr_stream_encode_bool(xdr, resp->common.err == nfserr_eof) < 0)
fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c:      return nfsd4_encode_bool(xdr, readdir->common.err == nfserr_eof);
fs/nfsd/nfsd.h: __be32                  err;    /* 0, nfserr, or nfserr_eof */
fs/nfsd/nfsd.h:#define  nfserr_eof              cpu_to_be32(NFSERR_EOF)
fs/nfsd/nfsxdr.c:               if (xdr_stream_encode_bool(xdr, resp->common.err == nfserr_eof) < 0)
fs/nfsd/vfs.c:          cdp->err = nfserr_eof; /* will be cleared on successful read */
fs/nfsd/vfs.c:  if (err == nfserr_eof || err == nfserr_toosmall)

A better interim approach might be to select an impossible value
for NFSERR_EOF, as is done for the internal NLM error status codes:

fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__drop_reply   cpu_to_be32(30000)
fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__deadlock     cpu_to_be32(30001)
fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__stale_fh     cpu_to_be32(30002)
fs/lockd/lockd.h:#define nlm__int__failed       cpu_to_be32(30003)

---

Let's add the "pragma public" directives in the patches where
they are first needed, instead of here. As subsequent patches
are modified, the need for these directives might vanish.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 03/20] nfs_common: add new NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new flags to the NFSv4 protocol, specifically NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis. These flags are intended to be used by later patches and allow Linux nfsd to omit certain information about directory cookies and ordering when providing a GET_DIR_DELEGATION response. The changes are based on a working group document that has been stable for over a year, but there is still some risk associated with the addition of these new flags.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 04/20] nfsd: allow nfsd to get a dir lease with an ignore mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the NFS server to request a directory lease with an ignore mask, enabling it to selectively ignore certain events such as file creation, deletion, and renaming. The patch introduces a new function `nfsd_notify_to_ignore` that converts notification types into an ignore mask, which is then applied to the file lease flags. This change enables more fine-grained control over the notifications provided to clients, addressing a limitation in the previous implementation where all events were always reported.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 05/20] nfsd: update the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a directory delegation in the NFS daemon. It introduces a new helper function, nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask(), which recalculates the fsnotify mask based on the leases present. The function is called when directory delegations are added or removed and ensures that concurrent calls to this function do not interfere with each other by using a mutex to serialize access to the lease state and mark update. This patch addresses potential issues where a recalculation working from stale data could clobber a concurrent update, leaving the mark missing required events.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 06/20] nfsd: make nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation bool return
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation to return a boolean value indicating whether callback processing should continue. The change is made to handle allocation failures during CB_NOTIFY operations, where processing should stop if an allocation fails.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 07/20] nfsd: add callback encoding and decoding linkages for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds encoding and decoding routines for CB_NOTIFY messages in the NFSv4 callback protocol. The encoding routine uses code generated by xdrgen to encode the message, while the decoding routine decodes the message using existing functions. Currently, the encoder is a stub that will be fleshed out in later patches. This patch also updates the nfsd state header and adds a new procedure for CB_NOTIFY to the NFSv4 callback procedures array.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 08/20] nfsd: use RCU to protect fi_deleg_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch protects the fi_deleg_file pointer in the NFS server code using RCU (Read-Copy Update) to prevent concurrent access issues during delegation teardown and lease removal. The patch annotates fi_deleg_file with __rcu, converts all accessors to use proper RCU primitives, and introduces rcu_dereference_protected() for safe dereferencing under certain conditions. This change prepares the code for a subsequent patch that will safely acquire a reference from the CB_NOTIFY callback path without holding the fi_lock lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 09/20] nfsd: add data structures for handling CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds data structures and callback operations to support directory delegation CB_NOTIFY in the NFS server (nfsd). It introduces two new structs: nfsd_notify_event, which carries fsnotify events for CB_NOTIFY, and nfsd4_cb_notify, which represents per-delegation state for notification handling. The patch also refactors alloc_init_deleg() into a common function with a pluggable sc_free callback and adds alloc_init_dir_deleg(), which allocates resources needed for CB_NOTIFY encoding. Additionally, it initializes the nfsd4_cb_notify_ops structure with done and release handlers that will be filled in when the notification path is implemented.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 10/20] nfsd: add notification handlers for dir events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for directory events in the NFS server (nfsd). It introduces a new notification handler for directory changes and creates a CB_NOTIFY request to handle these notifications. The patch also allocates an nfsd_notify_event object and hands off a reference to each delegation's CB_NOTIFY. If any errors occur during this process, it recalls any affected delegations. This change aims to improve the handling of directory events in the NFS server.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 11/20] nfsd: apply the notify mask to the delegation when requested
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the NFSv4 server to apply the requested notify mask to directory delegations when notifications are enabled by the client. If the client requests notifications without specifying NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND, no notifications will be offered as the server does not provide directory offset information required for classic notifications. The patch ensures that the lease acquisition sets the appropriate ignore mask based on the applied notify mask.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 12/20] nfsd: add helper to marshal a fattr4 from completed args
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function `nfsd4_encode_attr_vals` to the NFS server code, which marshals attribute values from completed arguments into a JSON object. The function breaks out the encoding of actual attr_vals field into a separate function, making the code more modular and easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 13/20] nfsd: allow nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() to work with no export
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change function to work even when there is no export available, which can occur in certain CB_NOTIFY callback scenarios. The change ensures that a delegation will not be granted on the V4 root, making this safe. This modification enables the function to operate correctly without requiring access to an svc_export.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 14/20] nfsd: send basic file attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to send basic inode information in a CB_NOTIFY event, including file attributes that can be acquired via GETATTR. The patch modifies the nfsd4_setup_notify_entry4 function to include inode attributes in the notify entry. It uses the vfs_getattr function to retrieve the inode attributes and then encodes them into the notify entry using the nfsd4_encode_attr_vals function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 15/20] nfsd: allow encoding a filehandle into fattr4 without a svc_fh
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the encoding of a filehandle into fattr4 without requiring a svc_fh. It does this by adding a new 'fhandle' field to struct nfsd4_fattr_args and populating it from the svc_fh in CB_NOTIFY callbacks. The patch also generalizes fh_append_mac() to operate on bare knfsd_fh structures rather than svc_fh.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 16/20] nfsd: add a fi_connectable flag to struct nfs4_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, fi_connectable, to the nfs4_file structure in the Linux kernel's NFSv4 server code. The flag indicates whether a filehandle is connectable or not, which is necessary for certain operations like CB_NOTIFY when subtree checking is enabled on the underlying export. The patch modifies the nfsd4_file_init function to set this flag based on the presence of subtree checking on the export where the directory delegation was acquired.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 17/20] nfsd: add the filehandle to returned attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new routine to compose a filehandle for CB_NOTIFY operations, which do not have access to the usual svc_export. The new routine uses only the parent filehandle and nfs4_file to fill out the fhandle field in the nfsd4_fattr_args. This allows the filehandle to be returned as an attribute in CB_NOTIFY responses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 18/20] nfsd: properly track requested child attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the way NFSd tracks and encodes child attributes in delegation requests. It introduces a union of requested and supported child attributes, ensuring that only relevant attributes are sent to clients. This change affects various parts of the NFSd codebase, including nfs4proc.c, nfs4state.c, and nfs4xdr.c. The patch aims to enhance the accuracy and efficiency of attribute encoding in delegation requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 19/20] nfsd: track requested dir attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch tracks the union of requested and supported directory attributes in the delegation, which will be used to ensure that only the relevant attributes are encoded when sending add/remove/rename updates. The patch modifies the nfsd4_verify function to update the delegation with the requested dir attributes, and introduces new macros for tracking dir attributes. This change is part of a larger effort to improve the handling of directory attribute changes in the NFSv4 protocol.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 20/20] nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support to CB_NOTIFY in the NFSv4 server to send notifications of directory attribute changes alongside other events such as add, remove, and rename operations. The server will still recall the delegation on a SETATTR operation, but these notifications are only sent for changes to child directories. The patch also addresses an issue where signed filehandles were not being appended correctly in certain export configurations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations, allowing clients to register interest in a directory and receive notifications about changes within it without losing its lease. The patches include fixes for various issues, such as RCU handling and encoding failures, and have been tested with pynfs but require a complete client-side implementation for final testing. The series builds on previous work on directory delegation and adds support for creates, unlinks, and renames, as well as sending updated directory attributes in notifications.

review_comments.raw_body:
RFC8881bis adds some new flags to GET_DIR_DELEGATION that later patches
will consume. In particular, Linux nfsd can't easily provide info about
directory cookies and ordering. The new flags allow it to omit that
information.

There is some risk here -- RFC8881bis is still a working group document,
and has been for years. The changes to directory delegations have been
stable for the last year or so however, so the hope is that those parts
won't change (much).

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the FL_IGN_DIR_* bits not being enabled when requesting a directory lease, and explained that they will be set based on the requested notification types in nfsd_get_dir_deleg(). The patch has been updated to include this fix.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about concurrent delegation adds and removes on the same directory causing stale snapshots of lease lists, which could lead to incorrect fsnotify marks. The author added a new mutex (nfm_recalc_mutex) to serialize callers and ensure that the mark is updated correctly. This fix will be included in the next version.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about CB_NOTIFY operations failing due to allocation issues, and responded by modifying the ->prepare callback operation to return true if processing should continue and false otherwise.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the encoding and decoding of CB_NOTIFY messages, explaining that they added routines for encoding and decoding these messages, but left the encoder as a stub to be fleshed out in later patches.
review_comments.raw_body:
Add routines for encoding and decoding CB_NOTIFY messages. These call
into the code generated by xdrgen to do the actual encoding and
decoding.

For now, the encoder is a stub. Later patches will flesh out the payload
encoding.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfs4callback.c | 46 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 fs/nfsd/state.h        |  8 ++++++++
 fs/nfsd/xdr4cb.h       | 12 ++++++++++++
 3 files changed, 66 insertions(+)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4callback.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4callback.c
index a3c46905fd47..ca4dd2f969eb 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4callback.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4callback.c
@@ -887,6 +887,51 @@ static void encode_stateowner(struct xdr_stream *xdr, struct nfs4_stateowner *so
 	xdr_encode_opaque(p, so->so_owner.data, so->so_owner.len);
 }
 
+static void nfs4_xdr_enc_cb_notify(struct rpc_rqst *req,
+				   struct xdr_stream *xdr,
+				   const void *data)
+{
+	const struct nfsd4_callback *cb = data;
+	struct nfs4_cb_compound_hdr hdr = {
+		.ident = 0,

review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the current implementation has a potential performance issue due to multiple buffer pools, and suggested using a per-ring freelist for each buffer size instead, which would simplify allocation and reduce overhead.

reviewer noted that the bufpool layer would still allow allocating and freeing single buffers, but questioned whether additional complexity or performance overhead for multi-bufpool support would be significant

review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think this is an issue.  Allocation should be as simple as
keeping per-ring freelist for each buffer size that would have all
free buffers across an arbitrary number of buf pools.

 I don't think it would add much additional complexity, given

---

To conclude: bufpools allow allocating/freeing multiple buffers
together.  The API is a superset, since allocating/freeing a single
buffer is still possible.   Additional complexity or performance for

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested an alternative implementation for managing multiple buffer pools, where each pool is represented by an array entry in a separate config-defined array, and proposed modifying the fuse_uring_ent_in_out function to set the corresponding indices.
review_comments.raw_body:
Really sorry for late replies.

Couldn't we make a config SQE define a max array size and then every
array entry would be something like

struct buf_entry {
	char *payload;
	int buf_pool_idx; /* which buf pool */
	int idx; /* index within the buf pool */	
}


And then in fuse_uring_ent_in_out you would need to set buf_pool_idx and
buf_idx


Avoids which buf pool still has free entries, but buf pools get added to
that array, which then would have its own head and tail. Obviously you
would need to do another pointer lookup to get the actual payload.

Obviously has one more pointer than just a single buf ring.


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the freelist concept, which allows for dynamic buffer pool addition and removal, is what Miklos was referring to, and confirmed that the uapi changes in this series are forward-compatible with multi-bufpools.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the max-size array works but if it's backed by a linked list
instead of a fixed array, bufpool add/remove can be more dynamic (eg
no fixed ceiling). I think this is the freelist concept Miklos was
talking about. I don't think the implementation details really matter
right now though - all the kernel-internal stuff could be changed and
figured out after this series.

The uapi changes this series adds are forward-compatible with
multi-bufpools. It adds a uint16_t buf_id to fuse_uring_ent_in_out,
and there's already a uint16_t padding field right after it that a
pool_id can occupy later.

Thanks,
Joanne

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called sdei_event_signal() to the Linux kernel's firmware/arm_sdei.c file. This function is a thin wrapper over the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call, which signals the software-signalled event (event 0) to another processor element (PE). The function takes two parameters: event_num and mpidr, where mpidr is the target PE's MPIDR affinity. The patch also updates the include/linux/arm_sdei.h file to add a declaration for sdei_event_signal(), and adds a new enum value SDEI_1_0_FN_SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL to the include/uapi/linux/arm_sdei.h file. This change is intended to make it safe to call from NMI or crash context, as it does not take any locks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds an additional escalation path to the smp_send_stop() function on arm64 architecture, which is used to stop a CPU that has become wedged and is ignoring interrupts. When the regular IPI (Inter-Processor Interrupt) and pseudo-NMI paths fail, the patch triggers an SDEI (System Description Event Interface) NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) as a last resort. This allows the system to recover from the wedged CPU by sending it an SDEI event that will eventually lead to its shutdown. The patch also introduces new functions and modifications to existing code to support this escalation path.

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch does not handle the case where the SDEI event is already pending when trying to escalate to it, and requested a check for this condition before triggering the SDEI event.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested combining the stop functionality into a single function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() to avoid synchronization issues across different stop paths, and provided an example implementation.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested that the patch could be improved by separately testing for SDEI availability before attempting to use it, and proposed modifying sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() to return void instead of printing a message.

Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the shared EVENT ID for 'backtrace' and 'stop', suggesting separate IDs would improve code understandability and allow elimination of the sdei_nmi_stop_mask variable.

Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the use of 'or' instead of 'copy' in sdei_nmi_stop_mask, suggesting a potential issue with the logic
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Tue, Jun 9, 2026 at 6:58 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

Can we combine everything into one function so we don't have to keep
all the different stop functionality in sync? Like this (untested):

void __noreturn arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(struct pt_regs *regs, bool die_on_crash)
{
  unsigned int cpu = smp_processor_id();
  bool crash = IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE) && crash_stop;

  /*
   * Use local_daif_mask() instead of local_irq_disable() to make sure
   * that pseudo-NMIs are disabled. The "stop" code starts with
   * an IRQ and falls back to NMI (which might be pseudo). If the IRQ
   * finally goes through right as we're timing out then the NMI could
   * interrupt us. It's better to prevent the NMI and let the IRQ
   * finish since the pt_regs will be better.
   */
  local_daif_mask();

  if (crash)
    crash_save_cpu(regs, cpu);

  set_cpu_online(cpu, false);

  sdei_mask_local_cpu();

  if (crash && die_on_crash)
    __cpu_try_die(cpu);

  /* just in case */
  cpu_park_loop();
}

Then in do_handle_IPI(), it's just:

  case IPI_CPU_STOP:
  case IPI_CPU_STOP_NMI:
    arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(get_irq_regs(), true);
    break;

...and from the SDEI code you pass "false" for "die_on_crash".

Perhaps when doing that you'd stop unconditionally getting the cpu in
do_handle_IPI() and just call it for `IPI_KGDB_ROUNDUP` since it would
now be the only consumer of that local variable.

FWIW, I'm not totally sure I followed the logic for why "die_on_crash"
needs to be "false" for the SDEI case, but I also am not terribly
familiar with KEXEC and crash-dumping kernels, since I've never
attempted to get that feature to work.

---

Perhaps it's being a bit pedantic, but it's a little weird that you're
printing a message that sounds like "I'm going to retry with SDEI"
after you've already done it. It feels like it would be nominally
cleaner (and more parellel with the pseudo-NMI case) if you could
separately test if SDEI is available. Then sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() would
just return void?

---

Do you have any reasoning for why you don't pick a separate EVENT ID
for "backtrace" vs. "stop". If you absolutely have to share an ID
because they're a limited resource then I guess it's fine, but it
would make the code easier to understand / reason about if they were
separate IDs.

If you had a separate EVENT ID, then it seems like you could
completely eliminate the (potentially large) `sdei_nmi_stop_mask`
variable, right? Any time a "STOP" event fires you can unconditionally
consider it to be a stop w/ no globals needed, right?

---

As per above, hopefully we can get rid of `sdei_nmi_stop_mask`. ...but
if we keep it, I'm curious why "or" and not "copy"?

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agreed to add a second parameter to arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() function to handle different scenarios, specifically for stop IPI handlers and SDEI handler.

Author clarified that the SDEI stop handler parks inside an event to prevent firmware from resuming a wedged context, which would be counterproductive.

Author responded to a concern about the local CPU being removed after the change, explaining that it's still used in two cases and thus not single-use.

The author addressed a concern about the SDEI NMI handler being called when sdei_nmi_active() is false, and fixed it by adding a predicate to check for active SDEI before calling sdei_nmi_stop_cpus().

The author acknowledged a reviewer's concern about sharing event IDs between backtrace and stop operations, explained that SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL can only signal event 0, and confirmed they replaced the cpumask with a single write-once flag to handle this limitation.

The author acknowledges that a previous concern about the mask being present has been resolved, and asks if there are any further comments.

The author addressed Doug Anderson's feedback about the need to handle the case where an SDEI event handler runs inside an unfinished SDEI event, which can wedge EL3 on some firmware. The author explained that this is a known issue and will be handled as a follow-up, but for now, they are focusing on getting the main functionality working.
review_comments.raw_body:
Okay. Look good to me. See the patch below.

void __noreturn arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(struct pt_regs *regs, bool die_on_crash)
The stop IPI handlers call it with die_on_crash=true, the SDEI handler
and panic_smp_self_stop() with false. Pretty much your sketch, with the
crash = IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE) && crash_stop discriminator inside.

---

It's not about kexec mechanics, it's about the SDEI dispatch state.

The SDEI stop handler parks inside an SDEI event that it deliberately
never completes \u2014 completing it makes firmware resume the wedged
context, which is the opposite of what we want. PSCI CPU_OFF from inside
that not-yet-completed event silently wedges EL3 on at least one
production firmware (still root-causing on the firmware side), so the
SDEI path saves the crashed context and parks instead of powering off.

The only consequence is that an SMP capture kernel can't re-online that
CPU. The dump itself is complete. I've left "power the SDEI-stopped CPU
off too" as a follow-up and called it out in the cover letter. The IPI
crash path is unaffected and still does CPU_OFF, exactly as before.

---

I kept the local cpu \u2014 after the change it's still used by the
IPI_KGDB_ROUNDUP case and the default: pr_crit(), so it didn't become
single-use.

---

Fixed. There's now a sdei_nmi_active() predicate; the rung tests it,
prints, then calls sdei_nmi_stop_cpus(), which is now void. It mirrors
the pseudo-NMI rung's check-then-act shape.

---

Separate event IDs aren't available: SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL only ever signals
event 0 \u2014 it's the one architecturally software-signalled event. Every
other event number is an interrupt-bound event that firmware has to
define and bind, which is the firmware dependency this series is
specifically trying not to add. So backtrace and stop are stuck sharing
event 0.

But you're right that the mask should go \u2014 just not via a second event. A
stop is terminal and system-wide (sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() is only reached
from smp_send_stop(), which never returns), so once a stop is requested
every later event-0 fire is a stop too. I replaced the cpumask with a
single write-once flag the handler reads; a backtrace that races in
after a stop has begun just stops that CPU, which is fine. So the
(potentially large) variable is gone.

---

It doesn't matter anymore. Mask is gone.

Thanks for the feedback! Any other comments?

--------------------------------8<-----------------------------------------------

From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>
Date: Tue, 2 Jun 2026 15:28:10 +0100
Subject: [PATCHv2.1 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last
 resort

A CPU wedged with interrupts masked ignores the stop IPI, and without
pseudo-NMI there is no NMI IPI to escalate to: a reboot proceeds with
the CPU still running, and a kdump misses its registers.

Add a third rung to smp_send_stop(): once the IPI (and pseudo-NMI IPI,
if enabled) rungs have run, signal SDEI event 0 at whatever stayed
online. Firmware delivers it regardless of the target's DAIF, so it
reaches a CPU a plain IPI cannot; the target acks by going offline,
which the caller already polls for.

Fold the stop bookkeeping into one arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(regs,
die_on_crash), shared by the stop IPI handlers, panic_smp_self_stop()
and the SDEI handler, replacing the near-duplicate local_cpu_stop() and
ipi_cpu_crash_stop(). @die_on_crash is the only difference: the IPI
handlers pass true and PSCI CPU_OFF the CPU on a crash stop so a capture
kernel can reclaim it; the SDEI handler and self-stop pass false and
park. The SDEI park is required, not conservative -- its handler runs
inside an SDEI event that is never completed (completing it resumes the
wedged context), and a CPU_OFF from that unfinished-event context wedges
EL3 on some firmware (left as a follow-up). The dump is unaffected; only
re-onlining the CPU in an SMP capture kernel is lost.

Suggested-by: Doug Anderson <dianders@chromium.org>
Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>

+/*
+ * Backtrace and stop both ride SDEI event 0. That is not a chosen economy:
+ * event 0 is the only architecturally software-signalled event -- the sole
+ * event SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL can target at an arbitrary PE. Every other event
+ * number is a firmware/platform interrupt-bound event, not something the
+ * kernel can raise cross-CPU, so a dedicated "stop" event would need
+ * firmware to define and bind it -- exactly the firmware dependency this
+ * driver sets out to avoid.
+ *
+ * Sharing one event means the handler must tell a stop apart from a
+ * backtrace. A stop is terminal and system-wide -- sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() is
+ * only reached from smp_send_stop() (reboot/halt/panic/kdump), which never

+ * stop too. A single write-once flag therefore carries as much as a
+ * per-CPU mask would: sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() sets it before signalling, and
+ * the handler reads a set flag as "stop this CPU" and a clear flag as
+ * "backtrace" (handled by nmi_cpu_backtrace(), which self-gates on the
+ * framework's backtrace mask). A backtrace fire that races in after a stop
+ * has begun just stops that CPU instead -- harmless, it is going down.
+ */
+static bool sdei_nmi_stopping;
+

 {
+	if (READ_ONCE(sdei_nmi_stopping)) {
+		/*
+		 * Never returns, and deliberately never completes the SDEI
+		 * event: SDEI_EVENT_COMPLETE has firmware restore the
+		 * interrupted context, which would land the CPU back in
+		 * the wedged loop (or in do_idle, which BUGs at
+		 * cpuhp_report_idle_dead once it sees itself offline).
+		 * Returning a modified pt_regs doesn't help --
+		 * arch/arm64/kernel/sdei.c::do_sdei_event only honours a PC
+		 * override via its IRQ-state heuristic and otherwise hands
+		 * EL3 its own saved-context slot back.
+		 *
+		 * Trade-off: EL3 retains ~one saved-context slot per parked
+		 * CPU until the next hardware reset (~hundreds of bytes per
+		 * CPU). Recoverability is unchanged versus an IPI-stopped
+		 * CPU: neither comes back without a reset.
+		 */
+		arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(regs, false);
+		/* unreachable */
+	}
+
 	/*
 	 * nmi_cpu_backtrace() no-ops unless this CPU's bit is set in the
 	 * global backtrace mask (driven by nmi_trigger_cpumask_backtrace()),
@@ -115,6 +163,33 @@ bool sdei_nmi_trigger_cpumask_backtrace(const cpumask_t *mask, int exclude_cpu)
 	return true;
 }
 
+bool sdei_nmi_active(void)
+{
+	return sdei_nmi_available;
+}
+
+/*
+ * Last rung of the stop escalation in smp_send_stop() (see
+ * arch/arm64/kernel/smp.c). The caller runs the regular stop IPI (and
+ * the pseudo-NMI stop IPI, where available) first; @mask holds whatever
+ * stayed online through those -- typically CPUs wedged with interrupts
+ * masked, unreachable by an IPI. Mark the stop in progress and signal
+ * event 0 at each target; a target acks by marking itself offline, which
+ * the caller polls for. The caller has already confirmed sdei_nmi_active().
+ */
+void sdei_nmi_stop_cpus(const cpumask_t *mask)
+{
+	unsigned int cpu;
+
+	WRITE_ONCE(sdei_nmi_stopping, true);
+
+	/* Publish the flag before the SMCs make targets read it */
+	smp_wmb();
+
+	for_each_cpu(cpu, mask)
+		sdei_nmi_fire(cpu);
+}

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture, using the SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) protocol. The goal is to enable interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked, which is necessary for certain debug and observability features such as system backtraces and crash dumps. The existing mechanisms, GICv3 pseudo-NMI and FEAT_NMI, have limitations or are not widely supported. This series adds a new delivery backend using SDEI, which incurs no additional cost on the hot path and can reach a CPU spinning with DAIF masked. It does not introduce a new hard-lockup detector but provides a way to reach a stalled CPU for existing detection methods.

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch does not include a Reviewed-by tag, which is required for kernel patches.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested combining the stop functionality into a single function, arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(), to avoid synchronization issues between different stop paths. He provided an example implementation and proposed changes to do_handle_IPI() and SDEI code.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested refactoring the code to separately check for SDEI availability before attempting to use it, and proposed returning void from sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() instead of printing a message.

The reviewer questioned the decision to share an SDEI event ID between 'backtrace' and 'stop' events, suggesting that separate IDs would improve code readability and allow for elimination of a global variable.

Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the use of 'or' instead of 'copy' in the sdei_nmi_stop_mask function, suggesting a potential issue with the logic.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Tue, Jun 9, 2026 at 6:58 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

Can we combine everything into one function so we don't have to keep
all the different stop functionality in sync? Like this (untested):

void __noreturn arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(struct pt_regs *regs, bool die_on_crash)
{
  unsigned int cpu = smp_processor_id();
  bool crash = IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE) && crash_stop;

  /*
   * Use local_daif_mask() instead of local_irq_disable() to make sure
   * that pseudo-NMIs are disabled. The "stop" code starts with
   * an IRQ and falls back to NMI (which might be pseudo). If the IRQ
   * finally goes through right as we're timing out then the NMI could
   * interrupt us. It's better to prevent the NMI and let the IRQ
   * finish since the pt_regs will be better.
   */
  local_daif_mask();

  if (crash)
    crash_save_cpu(regs, cpu);

  set_cpu_online(cpu, false);

  sdei_mask_local_cpu();

  if (crash && die_on_crash)
    __cpu_try_die(cpu);

  /* just in case */
  cpu_park_loop();
}

Then in do_handle_IPI(), it's just:

  case IPI_CPU_STOP:
  case IPI_CPU_STOP_NMI:
    arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(get_irq_regs(), true);
    break;

...and from the SDEI code you pass "false" for "die_on_crash".

Perhaps when doing that you'd stop unconditionally getting the cpu in
do_handle_IPI() and just call it for `IPI_KGDB_ROUNDUP` since it would
now be the only consumer of that local variable.

FWIW, I'm not totally sure I followed the logic for why "die_on_crash"
needs to be "false" for the SDEI case, but I also am not terribly
familiar with KEXEC and crash-dumping kernels, since I've never
attempted to get that feature to work.

---

Perhaps it's being a bit pedantic, but it's a little weird that you're
printing a message that sounds like "I'm going to retry with SDEI"
after you've already done it. It feels like it would be nominally
cleaner (and more parellel with the pseudo-NMI case) if you could
separately test if SDEI is available. Then sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() would
just return void?

---

Do you have any reasoning for why you don't pick a separate EVENT ID
for "backtrace" vs. "stop". If you absolutely have to share an ID
because they're a limited resource then I guess it's fine, but it
would make the code easier to understand / reason about if they were
separate IDs.

If you had a separate EVENT ID, then it seems like you could
completely eliminate the (potentially large) `sdei_nmi_stop_mask`
variable, right? Any time a "STOP" event fires you can unconditionally
consider it to be a stop w/ no globals needed, right?

---

As per above, hopefully we can get rid of `sdei_nmi_stop_mask`. ...but
if we keep it, I'm curious why "or" and not "copy"?

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agrees that the patch looks good and confirms it matches a previously proposed design.

The author addressed Doug Anderson's concern that the SDEI dispatch state causes issues with kexec mechanics, explaining that it's actually about parking the crashed context instead of powering off the CPU, which only affects SMP capture kernels' ability to re-online the CPU.

The author addressed a concern that the local cpu variable was being made single-use, explaining that it is still used in two cases and therefore did not need to be changed.

The author addressed a concern about the lack of a predicate to check if an SDEI NMI is active before stopping CPUs, and has added a new function sdei_nmi_active() that checks this condition.

The author acknowledged that event IDs aren't available for SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL and explained why they can't use separate event IDs, instead opting to share event 0 between backtrace and stop paths. They also removed the cpumask variable.

The author acknowledges that a previous concern about interrupt masking has been addressed and no longer applies, but requests further feedback.

Author addressed a concern about CPUs ignoring stop IPIs when interrupts are masked, acknowledged the need for an additional NMI delivery method to reach these CPUs, and explained that adding a third rung to smp_send_stop() using SDEI event 0 will allow firmware to deliver the interrupt regardless of the target's DAIF state. The author confirmed that this change is necessary to prevent kdump crashes from missing registers and agreed to fold stop bookkeeping into one function shared by all stop paths.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged need for additional NMI delivery method
review_comments.raw_body:
Okay. Look good to me. See the patch below.

void __noreturn arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(struct pt_regs *regs, bool die_on_crash)
The stop IPI handlers call it with die_on_crash=true, the SDEI handler
and panic_smp_self_stop() with false. Pretty much your sketch, with the
crash = IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE) && crash_stop discriminator inside.

---

It's not about kexec mechanics, it's about the SDEI dispatch state.

The SDEI stop handler parks inside an SDEI event that it deliberately
never completes \u2014 completing it makes firmware resume the wedged
context, which is the opposite of what we want. PSCI CPU_OFF from inside
that not-yet-completed event silently wedges EL3 on at least one
production firmware (still root-causing on the firmware side), so the
SDEI path saves the crashed context and parks instead of powering off.

The only consequence is that an SMP capture kernel can't re-online that
CPU. The dump itself is complete. I've left "power the SDEI-stopped CPU
off too" as a follow-up and called it out in the cover letter. The IPI
crash path is unaffected and still does CPU_OFF, exactly as before.

---

I kept the local cpu \u2014 after the change it's still used by the
IPI_KGDB_ROUNDUP case and the default: pr_crit(), so it didn't become
single-use.

---

Fixed. There's now a sdei_nmi_active() predicate; the rung tests it,
prints, then calls sdei_nmi_stop_cpus(), which is now void. It mirrors
the pseudo-NMI rung's check-then-act shape.

---

Separate event IDs aren't available: SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL only ever signals
event 0 \u2014 it's the one architecturally software-signalled event. Every
other event number is an interrupt-bound event that firmware has to
define and bind, which is the firmware dependency this series is
specifically trying not to add. So backtrace and stop are stuck sharing
event 0.

But you're right that the mask should go \u2014 just not via a second event. A
stop is terminal and system-wide (sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() is only reached
from smp_send_stop(), which never returns), so once a stop is requested
every later event-0 fire is a stop too. I replaced the cpumask with a
single write-once flag the handler reads; a backtrace that races in
after a stop has begun just stops that CPU, which is fine. So the
(potentially large) variable is gone.

---

It doesn't matter anymore. Mask is gone.

Thanks for the feedback! Any other comments?

From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>
Date: Tue, 2 Jun 2026 15:28:10 +0100
Subject: [PATCHv2.1 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last
 resort

A CPU wedged with interrupts masked ignores the stop IPI, and without
pseudo-NMI there is no NMI IPI to escalate to: a reboot proceeds with
the CPU still running, and a kdump misses its registers.

Add a third rung to smp_send_stop(): once the IPI (and pseudo-NMI IPI,
if enabled) rungs have run, signal SDEI event 0 at whatever stayed
online. Firmware delivers it regardless of the target's DAIF, so it
reaches a CPU a plain IPI cannot; the target acks by going offline,
which the caller already polls for.

Fold the stop bookkeeping into one arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(regs,
die_on_crash), shared by the stop IPI handlers, panic_smp_self_stop()
and the SDEI handler, replacing the near-duplicate local_cpu_stop() and
ipi_cpu_crash_stop(). @die_on_crash is the only difference: the IPI
handlers pass true and PSCI CPU_OFF the CPU on a crash stop so a capture
kernel can reclaim it; the SDEI handler and self-stop pass false and
park. The SDEI park is required, not conservative -- its handler runs
inside an SDEI event that is never completed (completing it resumes the
wedged context), and a CPU_OFF from that unfinished-event context wedges
EL3 on some firmware (left as a follow-up). The dump is unaffected; only
re-onlining the CPU in an SMP capture kernel is lost.

Suggested-by: Doug Anderson <dianders@chromium.org>
Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>

diff --git a/drivers/firmware/arm_sdei_nmi.c b/drivers/firmware/arm_sdei_nmi.c
index a82776e7b55a..b2a69be6008f 100644
--- a/drivers/firmware/arm_sdei_nmi.c
+++ b/drivers/firmware/arm_sdei_nmi.c
@@ -29,6 +29,11 @@

  *     dumps all reach interrupt-masked CPUs.
  *
+ *   - sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() \u2014 the last rung of smp_send_stop()'s
+ *     escalation (reboot/halt and the panic/kdump crash stop alike),
+ *     reaching CPUs that ignored the stop IPIs; on the kdump path the
+ *     wedged context is captured into the vmcore before the CPU parks.
+ *
  * Delivery uses the standard SDEI software-signalled event (event 0) and
  * SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL. We register a handler for event 0, enable it, and
  * poke a target CPU with sdei_event_signal(0, mpidr): firmware makes
@@ -59,8 +64,51 @@ static bool sdei_nmi_available;
 
 #define SDEI_NMI_EVENT			0
 
+/*
+ * Backtrace and stop both ride SDEI event 0. That is not a chosen economy:
+ * event 0 is the only architecturally software-signalled event -- the sole
+ * event SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL can target at an arbitrary PE. Every other event
+ * number is a firmware/platform interrupt-bound event, not something the
+ * kernel can raise cross-CPU, so a dedicated "stop" event would need
+ * firmware to define and bind it -- exactly the firmware dependency this
+ * driver sets out to avoid.
+ *
+ * Sharing one event means the handler must tell a stop apart from a
+ * backtrace. A stop is terminal and system-wide -- sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() is
+ * only reached from smp_send_stop() (reboot/halt/panic/kdump), which never
+ * returns -- so once a stop is requested, every later event-0 fire is a
+ * stop too. A single write-once flag therefore carries as much as a
+ * per-CPU mask would: sdei_nmi_stop_cpus() sets it before signalling, and
+ * the handler reads a set flag as "stop this CPU" and a clear flag as
+ * "backtrace" (handled by nmi_cpu_backtrace(), which self-gates on the
+ * framework's backtrace mask). A backtrace fire that races in after a stop
+ * has begun just stops that CPU instead -- harmless, it is going down.
+ */
+static bool sdei_nmi_stopping;
+
 static int sdei_nmi_handler(u32 event, struct pt_regs *regs, void *arg)
 {
+	if (READ_ONCE(sdei_nmi_stopping)) {
+		/*
+		 * Never returns, and deliberately never completes the SDEI
+		 * event: SDEI_EVENT_COMPLETE has firmware restore the
+		 * interrupted context, which would land the CPU back in
+		 * the wedged loop (or in do_idle, which BUGs at
+		 * cpuhp_report_idle_dead once it sees itself offline).
+		 * Returning a modified pt_regs doesn't help --
+		 * arch/arm64/kernel/sdei.c::do_sdei_event only honours a PC
+		 * override via its IRQ-state heuristic and otherwise hands
+		 * EL3 its own saved-context slot back.
+		 *
+		 * Trade-off: EL3 retains ~one saved-context slot per parked
+		 * CPU until the next hardware reset (~hundreds of bytes per
+		 * CPU). Recoverability is unchanged versus an IPI-stopped
+		 * CPU: neither comes back without a reset.
+		 */
+		arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(regs, false);
+		/* unreachable */
+	}
+
 	/*
 	 * nmi_cpu_backtrace() no-ops unless this CPU's bit is set in the
 	 * global backtrace mask (driven by nmi_trigger_cpumask_backtrace()),
@@ -115,6 +163,33 @@ bool sdei_nmi_trigger_cpumask_backtrace(const cpumask_t *mask, int exclude_cpu)
 	return true;
 }
 
+bool sdei_nmi_active(void)
+{
+	return sdei_nmi_available;
+}
+
+/*
+ * Last rung of the stop escalation in smp_send_stop() (see
+ * arch/arm64/kernel/smp.c). The caller runs the regular stop IPI (and
+ * the pseudo-NMI stop IPI, where available) first; @mask holds whatever
+ * stayed online through those -- typically CPUs wedged with interrupts
+ * masked, unreachable by an IPI. Mark the stop in progress and signal
+ * event 0 at each target; a target acks by marking itself offline, which
+ * the caller polls for. The caller has already confirmed sdei_nmi_active().
+ */
+void sdei_nmi_stop_cpus(const cpumask_t *mask)
+{
+	unsigned int cpu;
+
+	WRITE_ONCE(sdei_nmi_stopping, true);
+
+	/* Publish the flag before the SMCs make targets read it */
+	smp_wmb();
+
+	for_each_cpu(cpu, mask)
+		sdei_nmi_fire(cpu);
+}
+
